content.text: Google Cloud Vertex AI is Google's managed AI platform. Among other models, it hosts Claude — allowing you to call Claude through Google's infrastructure instead of Anthropic's servers. This matters when your organization uses Google Cloud Platform (GCP) and wants Claude to sit inside the same cloud environment as your other data and services.

If you are already storing data in Cloud Storage, running jobs in BigQuery, or deploying apps on Cloud Run, using Vertex AI means Claude stays in the same network and compliance boundary as everything else — no data needs to leave GCP to reach Claude.
Vertex AI is a marketplace inside Google Cloud. Anthropic puts Claude there so GCP customers can use it with GCP authentication, billing, and networking — without calling Anthropic's API directly.

attrs.alt: Comparison: Direct Anthropic API versus Google Cloud Vertex AI — what changes and what stays the same

content.text: Why Use Vertex AI Instead of the Direct API?
Both paths let you call Claude and get the same intelligent responses. The difference is in the plumbing:
Your requests and responses never leave Google Cloud's network — important for GDPR or HIPAA compliance
Claude usage appears on your Google Cloud invoice alongside other GCP costs
Use the same Google service accounts you use everywhere else — no separate Anthropic API key to manage
Latency, error rates, and token usage appear in Cloud Monitoring dashboards automatically
Claude runs in the same region as your Cloud Storage, BigQuery, and Cloud Run services — low-latency integration
If none of these matter for your project — you are just prototyping, or you use multiple cloud providers — the Direct Anthropic API is simpler to start with.
You need a Google Cloud project with the Vertex AI API enabled and a service account that has permission to call Vertex AI.

# Enable Vertex AI in your GCP project
gcloud services enable aiplatform.googleapis.com --project=YOUR_PROJECT_ID

# Create a service account for your app
gcloud iam service-accounts create my-claude-app   --project=YOUR_PROJECT_ID

# Grant Vertex AI access
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding YOUR_PROJECT_ID   --member="serviceAccount:my-claude-app@YOUR_PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com"   --role="roles/aiplatform.user"

# Download a credentials file (for local development only)
gcloud iam service-accounts keys create credentials.json   --iam-account=my-claude-app@YOUR_PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com

# Tell the SDK where to find your credentials
export GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="$(pwd)/credentials.json"
In production on GCP (Cloud Run, GKE, Compute Engine), the application receives credentials automatically from the compute platform — you do not need to download or manage a credentials file.

# Install the Anthropic SDK with Vertex AI support
pip install "anthropic[vertex]"
library needed for GCP authentication. The
client class is otherwise identical to the regular

import anthropic

# Connect to Vertex AI
client = anthropic.AnthropicVertex(
    region="us-central1",              # the GCP region to use
    project_id="YOUR_PROJECT_ID",      # your Google Cloud project
)

# Call Claude — same interface as the Direct API
message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8@20251101",  # Vertex model IDs include a version
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is Google Cloud Vertex AI in one sentence?"},
    ],
)

print(message.content[0].text)
The only two differences from a Direct API call are:
Vertex AI requires you to specify which version of a model you want, using an
suffix. This is a feature, not a quirk: it prevents your application from silently switching to a new model version when Anthropic releases an update.
Pin your model version and update it deliberately — check Vertex AI's Claude model release notes when you want to upgrade, test with your use case, then update the version string in your code.
Streaming works exactly the same on Vertex AI as on the Direct API:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.AnthropicVertex(
    region="us-central1",
    project_id="YOUR_PROJECT_ID",
)

with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-opus-4-8@20251101",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain cloud computing in simple terms."}],
) as stream:
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        print(text, end="", flush=True)
print()  # newline after the streamed response
in code, set them as environment variables — cleaner for production deployments:

import anthropic

# Reads project and region from environment variables
client = anthropic.AnthropicVertex()

message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8@20251101",
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello!"}],
)
print(message.content[0].text)
Choose the region closest to your users and where your data compliance boundary requires processing:
Most models available; best for US
Model availability varies by region. Newer models debut in
first and roll out to other regions over time. Check the Vertex AI Model Garden in the GCP console to confirm availability in your target region before deploying.
: Your company mandates that data stays within GCP
: You need Anthropic's inference to be covered by Google's DPA or BAA
: Your Cloud Run, BigQuery, or GKE workloads are already on GCP and you want Claude in the same network
: You have a committed GCP spend and want Claude usage to count toward that commitment

If you need the absolute latest model features immediately at launch, the Direct API gets them first — Vertex AI availability follows with a short lag. For advanced features like cross-region routing or Bedrock-specific guardrails, Amazon Bedrock is the alternative.
Vertex AI supports the same stateless multi-turn conversation pattern as the Direct API. You maintain the conversation history in your code and pass it with each request.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.AnthropicVertex(
    region="us-central1",
    project_id="YOUR_PROJECT_ID",
)

# Start with an empty conversation history
messages = []

def chat(user_input: str) -> str:
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_input})
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8@20251101",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=messages,
    )
    assistant_text = response.content[0].text
    messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": assistant_text})
    return assistant_text

# First turn
print(chat("I am learning about GCP. Where should I start?"))

# Second turn — Claude remembers the first exchange
print(chat("What about Vertex AI specifically?"))

# Third turn
print(chat("How does Claude fit into Vertex AI?"))
is independent — Claude has no memory of previous calls. By appending each turn to
yourself and passing the full list, you reconstruct the context on every request.
System prompts work identically on Vertex AI and the Direct API. Pass a
string to set Claude's role, persona, or behavioral constraints for the entire conversation.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.AnthropicVertex(
    region="us-central1",
    project_id="YOUR_PROJECT_ID",
)

message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8@20251101",
    max_tokens=512,
    system="You are a concise technical writer. Explain concepts in plain language, use examples, and keep answers under 150 words.",
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is a service account in GCP?"},
    ],
)
print(message.content[0].text)
parameter accepts either a plain string or a list of content blocks. The list form is needed for Prompt Caching (to mark the system prompt with
). For most beginners, a plain string is sufficient.
Network issues, quota limits, or invalid requests raise exceptions from the
package. The same exception types work for both the Direct API and Vertex AI:
Wait and retry; reduce request rate
Fix model ID, max_tokens, or messages format
Retry with backoff; report if persistent
object that shows how many tokens were used. Tracking this helps you estimate cost and debug unexpectedly large requests.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.AnthropicVertex(
    region="us-central1",
    project_id="YOUR_PROJECT_ID",
)

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8@20251101",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize GCP in three sentences."}],
)

print(response.content[0].text)
print()
print(f"Input tokens:  {response.usage.input_tokens}")
print(f"Output tokens: {response.usage.output_tokens}")
print(f"Total tokens:  {response.usage.input_tokens + response.usage.output_tokens}")

Cost = (input_tokens / 1,000,000) × input_price + (output_tokens / 1,000,000) × output_price. Check the Vertex AI pricing page for current Claude rates. Output tokens are priced higher than input tokens because generating text requires more compute than reading it.
The main reason to choose Vertex AI over the Direct API is the ability to integrate Claude natively with other GCP services. Three common beginner patterns:
Reading a File from Cloud Storage

from google.cloud import storage
import anthropic

storage_client = storage.Client()
vertex_client = anthropic.AnthropicVertex(
    region="us-central1",
    project_id="YOUR_PROJECT_ID",
)

# Read a document from GCS
bucket = storage_client.bucket("my-bucket")
blob = bucket.blob("documents/report.txt")
document_text = blob.download_as_text()

# Send it to Claude for summarization
response = vertex_client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8@20251101",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Summarize this document in 3 bullet points:\n\n{document_text}",
        }
    ],
)
print(response.content[0].text)

from google.cloud import bigquery
import anthropic

bq = bigquery.Client()
client = anthropic.AnthropicVertex(region="us-central1", project_id="YOUR_PROJECT_ID")

reviews = ["Great product!", "Broke after one day.", "Decent, but pricey."]

rows_to_insert = []
for review in reviews:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5@20251001",   # Haiku is cheaper for simple tasks
        max_tokens=10,
        system="Reply with exactly one word: positive, negative, or neutral.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": review}],
    )
    sentiment = response.content[0].text.strip().lower()
    rows_to_insert.append({"review": review, "sentiment": sentiment})

# Write results to BigQuery
table_id = "YOUR_PROJECT_ID.dataset.reviews"
errors = bq.insert_rows_json(table_id, rows_to_insert)
if not errors:
    print(f"Inserted {len(rows_to_insert)} rows")
The authentication setup differs between local development and deploying to GCP:
Service account key file or gcloud ADC
Set GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS or run gcloud auth application-default login
Assign service account to the Cloud Run service at deploy time
Bind Kubernetes SA to GCP SA with IAM annotation
Assign service account when creating the VM
On local machines, the simplest approach for development is Application Default Credentials: run
once in your terminal, and the Vertex AI SDK will pick up your personal GCP credentials automatically — no need to download a service account key file.

# Local dev: authenticate with your own Google account
gcloud auth application-default login

# Then your Python code just works — no GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS needed
python3 your_script.py
Vertex AI pricing for Claude is per token. As of late 2025, approximate rates (verify current prices in the GCP pricing documentation):

A typical API call with a 500-token prompt and 500-token response costs roughly 0.04 cents (Haiku) to 4.5 cents (Opus). At 1,000 calls per day, that is $0.40 to $45 per day. Use Haiku for classification, tagging, or short extraction tasks; reserve Opus for complex reasoning or long-form writing.
Set up GCP Budget Alerts on your project to receive an email when spending reaches 50%, 90%, and 100% of a monthly budget. This prevents surprise invoices during development.
: The service account does not have the
role on the project. Check with
and re-add the binding if missing.
: The model ID or version string is wrong, or the model is not yet available in your region. Check Vertex AI Model Garden in the console for the exact ID.
environment variable is missing and no
was passed. Set the env var or pass it explicitly.
: The SDK cannot find credentials. For local dev, run
or set the env var to the path of a service account key JSON file.
: The first call after a period of inactivity incurs a cold-start latency on the Vertex AI backend. This is normal; subsequent calls are faster.

The following script puts everything together: it reads a text file, calls Claude via Vertex AI, streams the response, and prints the final token usage. It is designed to run as-is with your project and credentials in place.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Complete Vertex AI + Claude example.
Usage:
  export ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_PROJECT_ID="YOUR_PROJECT_ID"
  export CLOUD_ML_REGION="us-central1"
  python3 vertex_example.py "path/to/document.txt"
"""
import sys
import time
import anthropic


def summarize_document(file_path: str) -> None:
    # Read the document
    with open(file_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        content = f.read()

    if len(content.strip()) == 0:
        print("Error: file is empty")
        sys.exit(1)

    word_count = len(content.split())
    print(f"Document: {file_path} ({word_count} words)")
    print("Calling Claude via Vertex AI...\n")

    # Create the Vertex AI client (reads project and region from env vars)
    client = anthropic.AnthropicVertex()

    start = time.perf_counter()

    # Stream the response
    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6@20250514",   # Sonnet: good balance for summarization
        max_tokens=1024,
        system="You are a precise technical summarizer. Extract the key points clearly.",
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": f"Summarize this document in 5 bullet points:\n\n{content}",
            }
        ],
    ) as stream:
        for text in stream.text_stream:
            print(text, end="", flush=True)

    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
    final = stream.get_final_message()

    print(f"\n\n--- Stats ---")
    print(f"Time:          {elapsed:.2f}s")
    print(f"Input tokens:  {final.usage.input_tokens}")
    print(f"Output tokens: {final.usage.output_tokens}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) != 2:
        print("Usage: python3 vertex_example.py <file.txt>")
        sys.exit(1)
    summarize_document(sys.argv[1])
environment variables so you do not need to hard-code your project ID.
retrieves the final usage stats after the stream completes.
Next Steps After Your First Call
Once you have made a successful Vertex AI call, consider these natural next steps:
— wrap your code in a Flask or FastAPI app and deploy with
for a scalable, serverless API endpoint
— log every request/response pair to Cloud Logging for audit trails and debugging
— create dashboards that show your Vertex AI request rates, latency percentiles, and error counts
— if you repeat the same large system prompt on every call, mark it with
to cut costs by up to 90% on the cached portion
Read the Vertex AI model card
— the model card for Claude on Vertex AI describes capability limits, content policies, and pricing in detail
One thing to do right now:

go to GCP Console → Vertex AI → Model Garden and search for "Claude." Look at which versions are available in your region. This is the canonical source for available model IDs and version strings — bookmark it.

Google Cloud Vertex AI lets you call Claude using GCP credentials and infrastructure — your data stays in the GCP region you choose, billing goes through Google Cloud, and you get native integration with GCP monitoring and services. The
SDK client is nearly identical to the Direct API client: swap the client constructor, add
suffix to your model ID. Everything else — messages, system prompts, streaming, tool use — works the same way.
